text: The velocity-time graph of a particle is a straight line rising from (0 s, 0 m/s) to (4 s, 8 m/s), after which the velocity stays constant at 8 m/s until 6 s. The total displacement in 6 s is:
explanation: Area under v-t graph = displacement. 0-4 s (triangle): ½×4×8 = 16 m. 4-6 s (rectangle): 8×2 = 16 m. Total = 32 m. Option (3). Trap: 16 m ignores the constant-velocity part; 48 m treats the first part as a rectangle.

text: A body is projected vertically upward with 30 m/s (g = 10 m/s²). The distance it covers during the 3rd second of its motion is:
explanation: Distance in the nth second: s_n = u − (g/2)(2n−1) = 30 − 5(2×3−1) = 30 − 25 = 5 m. Option (1). Trap: 25 m uses (2n−1) wrongly as the full term.

text: A projectile gives the same horizontal range for launch angles 30° and 60°. If the maximum height for the 30° launch is 15 m, the maximum height for the 60° launch is:
explanation: Max height H ∝ sin²θ (same u). H(60°)/H(30°) = sin²60°/sin²30° = (3/4)/(1/4) = 3, so H = 3×15 = 45 m. Option (3). Trap: 5 m divides instead of multiplying.

text: Two masses 3 kg and 2 kg hang from the ends of a light string over a frictionless pulley (Atwood machine). Taking g = 10 m/s², the acceleration of the system is:
A block slides down a rough incline of 37° (sin 37° = 0.6, cos 37° = 0.8) with coefficient of kinetic friction 0.5. Its acceleration (g = 10 m/s²) is:
explanation: a = g(sinθ − μ cosθ) = 10(0.6 − 0.5×0.8) = 10(0.6 − 0.4) = 2 m/s². Option (1). Trap: 6 m/s² forgets friction (only g sinθ).

text: A 10 kg block on a frictionless floor is pulled by a 50 N force directed 37° above the horizontal (cos 37° = 0.8). Its horizontal acceleration is:
explanation: Only the horizontal component drives motion: F_x = 50 cos 37° = 40 N, a = 40/10 = 4 m/s². Option (2). Trap: 5 m/s² uses the full 50 N.

text: A variable force F = 2x newton (x in metres) acts on a body along the x-axis. The work done in moving the body from x = 0 to x = 3 m is:
explanation: W = ∫₀³ 2x dx = [x²]₀³ = 9 J. Option (3). Trap: 6 J just evaluates 2x at x = 3.

text: A small body of mass m is to just complete a vertical circle of radius 2 m. The minimum speed it must have at the topmost point (g = 10 m/s²) is:
explanation: At the top, gravity supplies the centripetal force: mg = mv²/R → v = √(gR) = √(10×2) = √20 m/s. Option (2).

text: A uniform disc and a uniform ring have equal mass and equal radius. The ratio of their moments of inertia (disc : ring) about the central axis perpendicular to their planes is:
explanation: Disc: I = ½MR²; ring: I = MR². Ratio = ½ : 1 = 1 : 2. Option (2).

text: A solid sphere rolls without slipping on a horizontal surface. The fraction of its total kinetic energy that is rotational is:
explanation: For a solid sphere I = (2/5)mR² and ω = v/R, so KE_rot = ½Iω² = (1/5)mv² and KE_total = ½mv² + (1/5)mv² = (7/10)mv². Fraction = (1/5)/(7/10) = 2/7. Option (2). Trap: 5/7 is the translational fraction.

text: A wheel of moment of inertia 2 kg·m² starts from rest and reaches an angular speed of 10 rad/s in 5 s under a constant torque. The torque applied is:
explanation: α = Δω/Δt = 10/5 = 2 rad/s²; τ = Iα = 2×2 = 4 N·m. Option (2).

text: The acceleration due to gravity at a height equal to the radius of the Earth above its surface (surface value g = 10 m/s²) is:
explanation: g' = g/(1 + h/R)². At h = R: g' = g/(1+1)² = g/4 = 2.5 m/s². Option (1). Trap: 5 m/s² uses g/2 (forgets the square).

text: A satellite orbits the Earth in a circular orbit of radius r with period T. If an identical satellite is placed in a circular orbit of radius 4r, its period is:
explanation: Kepler's third law: T ∝ r^(3/2). (4)^(3/2) = 8, so the new period is 8T. Option (3). Trap: 16T uses T ∝ r².

text: Two wires of the same material have lengths in the ratio 1 : 2 and radii in the ratio 2 : 1. When stretched by the same force, the ratio of their extensions is:
explanation: ΔL = FL/(AY) and A ∝ r², so ΔL ∝ L/r². Ratio = (L₁/r₁²)/(L₂/r₂²) = (1/4)/(2/1) = 1/8, i.e. 1 : 8. Option (1).

text: The excess pressure inside one soap bubble is 4 times that inside another. The ratio of the radius of the first bubble to that of the second is:
explanation: Excess pressure in a soap bubble = 4T/r, so P ∝ 1/r. If P₁ = 4P₂ then r₁ = r₂/4, i.e. r₁ : r₂ = 1 : 4. Option (1).

text: A Carnot engine operates between a source at 400 K and a sink at 300 K. Its efficiency is:
explanation: η = 1 − T_sink/T_source = 1 − 300/400 = 0.25 = 25%. Option (2). Trap: 75% is T_sink/T_source.

text: An ideal monatomic gas is heated at constant pressure. The ratio of the work done by the gas to the heat supplied to it is:
explanation: At constant pressure W = nRΔT and Q = nC_pΔT with C_p = (5/2)R for a monatomic gas. W/Q = R/C_p = R/((5/2)R) = 2/5. Option (1).

text: At the same temperature, the ratio of the rms speeds of oxygen (M = 32) to hydrogen (M = 2) molecules is:
explanation: v_rms ∝ 1/√M. v(O₂)/v(H₂) = √(2/32) = √(1/16) = 1/4, i.e. 1 : 4. Option (1). Trap: 1 : 16 is the mass ratio, not its square root.

text: The heat required to convert 10 g of ice at 0°C completely into water at 0°C (latent heat of fusion = 80 cal/g) is:
explanation: Q = mL = 10×80 = 800 cal. Option (2). (No temperature change occurs during melting.)

text: A particle executes SHM of amplitude A. At what displacement from the mean position is its kinetic energy equal to its potential energy?
explanation: PE = ½kx² and KE = ½k(A²−x²). Setting KE = PE gives A²−x² = x² → x = A/√2. Option (2).

text: A spring of force constant k with a mass m attached has time period T. The spring is cut into two equal halves and the same mass m is attached to one half. The new time period is:
explanation: Cutting a spring in half doubles its force constant (k' = 2k). T = 2π√(m/k), so T' = 2π√(m/2k) = T/√2. Option (2). Trap: √2 T assumes k halves.

text: A pipe closed at one end has a fundamental frequency of 200 Hz. An open pipe of the same length has a fundamental frequency of:
explanation: Closed pipe: f = v/4L. Open pipe: f = v/2L = 2×(v/4L). So the open pipe's fundamental is twice that of the closed pipe = 400 Hz. Option (3).

text: At the midpoint of the line joining two equal positive point charges, the electric field and the electric potential are respectively:
explanation: The two equal fields point in opposite directions and cancel → E = 0. Potentials are scalars and add, so V = 2kq/(d/2) ≠ 0. Hence field zero, potential non-zero. Option (2).

text: A parallel-plate capacitor is charged by a battery, after which the battery is disconnected. A dielectric slab of constant k = 2 is then fully inserted between the plates. The ratio of the final stored energy to the initial stored energy is:
explanation: Battery disconnected → charge Q is constant. U = Q²/(2C); inserting the dielectric makes C → 2C, so U → U/2. Ratio = 1/2. Option (2). Trap: if the battery stayed connected, U would double.

text: The work done in moving a charge q between two points lying on the same equipotential surface is:
explanation: Points on an equipotential surface have the same potential, so ΔV = 0 and W = qΔV = 0. Option (1).

text: Three capacitors of 2 µF, 3 µF and 6 µF are connected in series. Their equivalent capacitance is:
explanation: 1/C = 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/6 = (3+2+1)/6 = 1, so C = 1 µF. Option (1). Trap: 11 µF is the parallel (sum) result.

text: A wire of resistance R is stretched uniformly so that its length doubles (volume unchanged). Its new resistance is:
explanation: R = ρL/A. Doubling L at constant volume halves A, so R' = ρ(2L)/(A/2) = 4×ρL/A = 4R. (Equivalently R ∝ L² at constant volume.) Option (2). Trap: 2R only scales the length.

text: A cell of emf 10 V and internal resistance 1 Ω is connected across an external resistance of 4 Ω. The terminal voltage of the cell is:
explanation: I = emf/(R+r) = 10/(4+1) = 2 A. Terminal voltage V = emf − Ir = 10 − 2×1 = 8 V. Option (2).

text: A 60 W and a 100 W bulb, both rated for 220 V, are connected in series across a 220 V supply. Which bulb glows brighter?
explanation: Rated resistance R = V²/P, so the 60 W bulb has the higher resistance. In series the current is the same, and power dissipated = I²R, so the higher-resistance 60 W bulb glows brighter. Option (1). The intuitive answer (100 W) is the trap.

text: A proton moving at 1×10⁶ m/s enters a magnetic field of 0.5 T perpendicular to its velocity. The magnitude of the magnetic force on it (e = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C) is:

Two charged particles of equal charge but masses m and 4m, having the same kinetic energy, enter a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to their velocities. The ratio of the radii of their circular paths is:
explanation: r = mv/(qB) = √(2mK)/(qB), so r ∝ √m for equal K and q. r₁ : r₂ = √m : √(4m) = 1 : 2. Option (1).

text: The magnetic field at the centre of a circular current loop is B. Keeping the current the same, the radius of the loop is doubled. The new magnetic field at the centre is:
explanation: B = µ₀I/(2R), so B ∝ 1/R. Doubling R halves the field → B/2. Option (2).

text: The magnetic flux through a 100-turn coil changes from 0.2 Wb to 0.6 Wb in 0.4 s. The magnitude of the induced emf is:
explanation: emf = N·|ΔΦ/Δt| = 100×(0.6−0.2)/0.4 = 100×1 = 100 V. Option (1). Trap: 40 V forgets the number of turns... actually 0.4/0.4=1, so omitting N gives 1 V; 40 V multiplies ΔΦ by N without dividing by time.

text: A series LCR circuit has R = 30 Ω and, at the driving frequency, X_L = X_C = 40 Ω. The impedance of the circuit is:
explanation: X_L = X_C means the circuit is at resonance, so Z = R = 30 Ω. Option (1). Trap: 50 Ω = √(30²+40²) ignores the cancellation of reactances.

text: An ideal transformer steps 220 V down to 22 V. If the primary has 1000 turns, the number of turns in the secondary is:
explanation: N_s/N_p = V_s/V_p → N_s = 1000×(22/220) = 100. Option (1). Trap: 10000 inverts the ratio.

text: The refractive index of a medium is 2. The critical angle for total internal reflection at its interface with air is:
explanation: sin C = 1/n = 1/2, so C = 30°. Option (1).

text: Two thin lenses of focal lengths +10 cm and −20 cm are placed in contact. The focal length of the combination is:
explanation: 1/F = 1/f₁ + 1/f₂ = 1/10 + 1/(−20) = (2−1)/20 = 1/20 → F = +20 cm. Option (1). Trap: +6.67 cm treats both focal lengths as positive.

text: An object is placed at the centre of curvature (2F) of a concave mirror. The image formed is:
options: real, inverted and of the same size
explanation: For an object at C (2F) of a concave mirror, the image forms at C: real, inverted and of the same size. Option (2).

text: In Young's double-slit experiment the screen distance is doubled and the slit separation is halved. The fringe width becomes:
explanation: Fringe width β = λD/d. Doubling D and halving d gives β' = λ(2D)/(d/2) = 4λD/d = 4β. Option (2).

text: Light of photon energy 3 eV falls on a metal of work function 2 eV. The maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is:
explanation: Einstein's photoelectric equation: KE_max = hν − φ = 3 − 2 = 1 eV. Option (1). Trap: 5 eV adds instead of subtracting.

text: The energy of the electron in the second excited state of the hydrogen atom is:
explanation: The second excited state is n = 3. E_n = −13.6/n² = −13.6/9 = −1.51 eV. Option (3). Trap: −3.4 eV (n = 2) misreads 'second excited state' as n = 2.

text: A radioactive sample has a half-life of 10 days. The fraction of the original nuclei remaining undecayed after 30 days is:
explanation: Number of half-lives = 30/10 = 3, so fraction remaining = (1/2)³ = 1/8. Option (3).

text: If the kinetic energy of a particle is increased to four times its original value, its de Broglie wavelength becomes:
explanation: λ = h/p = h/√(2mK), so λ ∝ 1/√K. Quadrupling K halves λ → λ/2. Option (1).

text: When a p-n junction diode is forward biased, the width of its depletion region:
explanation: Forward bias opposes the built-in potential, narrowing the depletion region. Option (2). (Reverse bias would widen it.)

text: The two inputs of a NAND gate are joined together to form a single input. The resulting gate behaves as a:
explanation: With both inputs equal to A, the output is NOT(A·A) = NOT(A). So the gate acts as a NOT (inverter). Option (3).
